title: Economic profile: Gloucester County, New Brunswick - Canada.ca
content:
## Economic profile: Gloucester County, New Brunswick
Gloucester County Footnote 1 has a population of 78,256, consisting of 29% seniors (65 and older), 61% working-age adults (15 to 64) and 11% children (under 15).
The key sectors of the economy in Gloucester County, which account for 46% of the jobs in the region, are
- health care and social assistance
There are 31,155 employed residents, of which 2.0% are immigrants. Footnote 2
The economy generates a total of $3.5 billion in household and business incomes, which amounts to $44,943 per resident.

### General age structure – 2021
% children (under 15)
% working-age adults (15 to 64)
% of seniors (65 and older)
In order to support local services such as health care and social programs and maintain a good standard of living for everyone in the region, it is essential to have more working-age adults than seniors. Gloucester County has fewer working-age adults than seniors compared to the average in New Brunswick.
From 2011 to 2021, Gloucester County experienced
- a 0.6% increase in population, compared to a 3.3% increase in the province
- a 54% increase in the population’s proportion of seniors, compared to a 39% increase in the province
- a 12% decrease in the population’s proportion of working-age adults, compared to an 8.2% decrease in the province

### Population structure – Changes from 2011 to 2021
Changes in the region
Changes in the province
Proportion of children
Proportion of working-age adults
Proportion of seniors
By 2031, without immigration, Footnote 3 the region could experience Footnote 4
- a 7.8% decrease in population
- a 14% decrease in the population’s proportion of working-age adults, which could limit the availability and quality of local services
- a further increase in the population’s proportion of seniors by 31%, potentially changing the demand for local services, including health care and long-term care

### No immigration – Possible changes from 2021 to 2031
Changes in the region
Changes in the province
Proportion of children
Proportion of working-age adults
Proportion of seniors
The region relies heavily on working-age adults to keep local services running smoothly and to maintain a good standard of living and economic growth. Immigrants can help by filling job gaps and bringing new skills and ideas, which keeps the local economy strong.
From 2011 to 2021, Gloucester County experienced
- a 6.0% decrease in workforce Footnote 5 size, compared to a 1.5% decrease in the province
- a 102% increase in the number of immigrant workers, compared to a 58% increase in the province
- a total of 9,133 retirements, Footnote 6 which amounts to 25% of the 2021 workforce, compared to 21% in the province
- a change in the average income per resident from local jobs relative to the province, which is 10% lower than the average in New Brunswick in 2021

By 2031, without immigration, Footnote 8 the region could experience Footnote 9
- a 47% decrease in workforce size
- a 42% decrease in the number of jobs
- an increase in the proportion of seniors in the population from 29% to 38%

## Supporting the community’s French-speaking population
Francophone immigration outside Quebec plays an important role in enhancing the vitality and economic development of Francophone minority communities across the country.
In Gloucester County, the communities of Chaleur (Belle-Baie, Bathurst and the Pabineau Mi'kmaq First Nation) and northeastern Acadia (Caraquet, Rivière du Nord and Hautes-Terres) are part of the Welcoming Francophone Communities initiative launched by Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada. This initiative supports the economic and socio-cultural integration of French-speaking newcomers. New Brunswick’s Acadian Peninsula, which includes Caraquet, Lamèque Island, Shippagan, Hautes-Terres, Tracadie and Neguac, is also one of six communities in Canada participating in the Francophone Community Immigration Pilot , a community-driven pilot designed to spread the benefits of economic immigration to smaller communities and increase the number of French-speaking newcomers in Francophone minority communities outside of Quebec.
In total, 89% of Gloucester County’s population speaks French, with 81% speaking French as a first language and 7.9% as a second language.
Unless otherwise noted, all statistics are from the 2011, 2016 or 2021 editions of the Statistics Canada Census, and all calculations are from the Canadian Centre for Economic Analysis.
